title: An Abundance Foreign Policy: Rebooting US Industrial Capacity and National Security
content:
A core premise of modern political theory is that much of the public anger driving populist movements stems from the devastating loss of high-wage jobs and rising consumer prices. Theorists suggest that if the United States can successfully reboot its public sector for the 21st century, a more cohesive and steady democracy will inevitably emerge. However, this concept of 'abundance' is no longer just an adjunct to domestic economic policy; it has rapidly evolved into a crucial national security doctrine. Formidable and flexible industrial capacity, built both at home and through reliable international allies, is now required to dissuade adversaries from economic and military aggression. Unfortunately, the current ossified American industrial base remains ill-equipped to produce the advanced technologies and munitions needed for comprehensive global deterrence.

To address this vulnerability, a tight focus on clearing supply chain bottlenecks and building swifter production capabilities could make the country significantly more secure. Key elements of this policy thinking have already gained strong traction among recent presidential administrations, which have actively worked to safeguard the American technological edge. An 'abundance foreign policy' would turbocharge these existing efforts by fine-tuning procurement machinery to scale up output in critical construction and advanced manufacturing sectors. Under this approach, the government would actively enlist private sector experts to find inventive ways to surmount output constraints, such as lead times and factory capacity limits. Recent episodes, like a major solar company furloughing workers due to minor customs delays, perfectly illustrate the urgent need to overhaul these domestic supply chains.
